>rc has a sitdown with a famous corporate raider, Carl Icahn in october 2022

>rc offers 400 million for BBBY in December 2022

>pitchbook (best M&A software suite in the world, costing 5 figures + per year) lists BBBY(now known as BBBYQ or DK-Butterfly-1) as sold via a leveraged buyout on January 13 2023

>a default on the ABL with JPM happened on the same day that pitchbook shows a leveraged buyout, the terms of that ABL specify that a default is triggered if a change of control happens, a change of control is defined as an acquisition of 40% or more of the voting power by any person or group.

>one month later, February 2023 RC tweets “someone tell the government to stop shooting down my balloons”, alluding to bad actors delaying the Bed Bath m&a

>the GameStop CEO then became a BBBYQ chapter 11 creditor and debtor

>Larry cheng (who helped RC build Chewy, and is a current boardmember at GameStop, and a close personal friend to RC) went onto theppshow podcast (which is dedicated to BBBY recovery theories) 8 months after BBBY filed for chapter 11, and 2 months after shares were cancelled

>OverStock - who is the most famous company to battle illegal naked shorts, who even released a crypto dividend 5 years ago - purchased the BBBY IP, and owns 55% of TZero - the only approved blockchain entity who can issue debt instruments and securities

>OverStock - whos now traded under ticker BBBY - has issued warrants with dates that match GME's warrants, and with a CUSIP-6 that matches BBBYQ's CUSIP-6, proving BBBYQ has already begun m&a activity with BBBY


REQUIRED READING: bbbwhy.com

If you are about to be deployed they are likely to crash the stock market soon. Even do a rage dump on the good stuff to crash the economy to get more guys like you or enlist or do another tour of duty. A legit criticism of anti war activists. Government is married to the banks, big biz, and military industrial complex. They will screw you and other young men over to get more cannon fodder.

Wouldn't be surprised if you were told to invest in the stock market to be a bag holder like all those college kids who were told they were all going to get a high paying jobs after college. Good advice if you time it right. But a bad idea for most things right now. Especially if you are stuck in a order following mind set. Not a low trust dangerous neighborhood mind set.

If you get back home alive I would recommend reading about Samuel Benner and Nikolai Kondratiev. The latter even predicted when wars are likely to break out with the timing of stock market crashes/bottom of bear markets. Sadly active duty soldiers will rarely have down time to put money into the stock market at the best entry points. But will have the best opportunities to sell high. This is why so many generals are rich. Not just getting some insider information. They got money to put in during their early careers and from being good strategists. Most low ranks will leave poor when they get out.

>>62448624

62448689

62448696
>>62448689
Boomers paid 20%

62448701
>>62447286
>2nd house for 425k around 31.
>2nd house is worth 900k now
What happened is their dollars lost half the purchasing power.
BTW they didn't but the house for $425k. They bought it for that plus whatever they pay in interest over time. Which is very likely to be closer to the amount they sold it for later.
It's nice to build equity, but don't be a fool.

62449002
>>62447246
>I have $180k sitting in an S&P500 index fund and every normie I meet says I'm making a mistake by not getting a mortgage ASAP because housing will only get more expensive.
>What is the /biz/ philosophy on this advice? I'm a rentoid and currently pay only around $1000 a month.
I'm in a similar position to you and unless you have a lifestyle reason to need a house it doesn't make financial sense right now IMO. Look at it financially, houses cost probably on the ballpark of 300-350k, let's just assume 325k median in a low to medium cost area, that's a 145k loan, at 6.25% interest rate the annual loss of interest only of 9k on year 1 and 8.9k on year 2 contrasted with an annual net loss currently of 12k a year renting but you also dont have other costs like property taxes, insurance, and maintenance costs that should be baked into rent but are not going to baked into that interest loss of yhe house so add those in too, where i live I'd pay 6k prop tax, 1k insurance, and rule of thumb is to budget 1-3% of home value so 3.25k-9.8k. To build on the case if you have money in the SP500 it has returned 10.5% annually (18.9k) or if you want to play it safe 4.5% in CDs (8.1k). That said, it depends on how long you are looking to buy a house, how risk tolerant you are, and if your landlord wants to increase rents. Run those numbers down a spreadsheet and see what makes sense for you in your area but i have come to the conclusion of letting my money ride in the market for the time being knowing the risk that I could get burned.

item.content:encoded:

Open thread • Replies: 21 • Updated: 2026-07-05 23:03 UTC

OP

Something like 70% of the American public has their money in the stock market.

Index fund investors (“Bogleheads bro!”) want you to believe that they’re all going to be rich because they put $500 a month into the market. This would essentially mean every single American would become rich just by DCAing into the US stock market.

Index investing is dead. The ONLY reason it worked in the past is because like 5% of Americans owned stocks. The ones that got in first benefited from everyone else piling in, exactly like Bitcoin.

Look at Bitcoin, all the Bitcoin retards made the same mistake of telling people it’ll just go up and up forever, now we’ve plateaued for the last 5 years and the Bitcoin trade is dead.

The only winners going forward will be good active investors. You’ve been warned.

No they won't - they're currently unlocking around 18 MILLION tokens per quarter. of that 18 million they sell around 15 million and put 3 million into their "reserve"
They don't actually "buy back" the tokens that go into the reserve

Then implied "we'll release staking v1 so everyone can stake" then completely backtracked on it.

They promised "everyone will pay for data feeds in link, or any payment will be converted to link"
Then it turned to be "actually we only accept Visa or Mastercard via Stripe"

They promised "we are going to buy back LINK for a strategic reserve"
Then it turned out that they were buying them from themselves at a zero cost

>But for it to compound you have to have a large volume of shares.
Nope. It will still compound at the same rate even with 1 share, as long as your fund supports fractional shares for DRIP.
>invest in mutual funds and etfs.
This is a very good idea for diversification, but does not matter for compounding.
>But those are extremely expensive
Only if you buy the ones that are expensive. While Vanguard's VOO is $684/share, Schwab's SWPPX is only $19. Both track the S&P 500 index. Vanguard supports fractional shares for DRIP. Schwab's SWPPX is cheap because Schwab doesn't like its funds to have a high purchase price so they do stock splits whenever the price gets too high (SWPPX recently had a 6:1 split), and it also has fractional shares for DRIP. Schwab's SCHG (large-cap growth ETF) is only $34/share (recently had a 4:1 stock split).

item.title: Behold the Golden Crab in the room — 16 replies — 22.7/hr

Behold the Golden Crab in the room

Open thread • Replies: 16 • Updated: 2026-07-05 22:35 UTC

OP

If you bought BTC at the end of 2017 you had purchased the value equivalent of around 15 oz of gold.
If you sold that BTC right now you could purchase... 15 oz of gold.
That's 9 years of bag holding to break even.
For most of the past 5 years, if you purchased Bitcoin, you actually lost value in gold.
What have we learned?
>bitcoin sucks for growing wealth
>fiat is garbage
>gold is the only real store of value of these three
